mnemonics.title: RPC Flow Order
mnemonics.content: CABS: Client initiates, Application calls stub, Stub marshals, Server receives and executes, Results travel back through Stub to Application

mnemonics.title: RPC Layer Names
mnemonics.content: ACRS: Application layer, Client Stub, RPC Runtime, Server Stub - remember 'Always Call Remote Server' reversed

mnemonics.title: RPC Issues Remembered
mnemonics.content: FANS: Failure handling, Authentication, Network latency, Synchronous vs Asynchronous - these are key design considerations

acronyms.usage: A protocol for client-server communication in distributed systems
Data serialization format used by Sun RPC for machine-independent data exchange
Language used to define the interface between client and server in RPC
A framework for building distributed systems including DCE RPC
Modern open-source RPC framework using HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers

acronyms.expansion: Extensible Markup Language Remote Procedure Call
acronyms.usage: RPC protocol using XML to encode calls and HTTP for transport

acronyms.expansion: JavaScript Object Notation Remote Procedure Call
acronyms.usage: Lightweight RPC protocol using JSON for data serialization

visualTips.visualization: Draw a vertical line dividing client and server sides. Show arrows from Client App → Client Stub → Network → Server Stub → Server App. Annotate each arrow with marshalling/unmarshalling operations.

Create a layered diagram with 5 horizontal layers: Application at top and bottom, Stubs in middle, RPC Runtime in center connecting both stubs like a bridge, with arrows showing data flow through layers.

Draw two boxes side by side representing Client Stub and Server Stub. Inside each, show the 3-step process: Marshal params → Send/Receive → Unmarshal results. Connect them with a bidirectional arrow labeled 'Network'.
Use a flowchart: Client → Directory Service (arrow labeled 'lookup') → Server Location → Client (arrow labeled 'handle'). Show how handle enables subsequent calls.

analogies.analogy: RPC is like ordering food from a restaurant. You (client) call with your order (parameters), the waiter (stub) takes it to the kitchen (server), the chef prepares it (executes procedure), and the waiter brings back your food (results). You don't need to know how the kitchen works.
Client and Server stubs are like translators in a meeting between people speaking different languages. They convert your words (data) into a format the other person understands (network format).
RPC Runtime is like a postal service handling delivery, tracking, and retries when packages (messages) are lost, while you just send and receive letters (procedure calls).
